primary_diagnosis.name: Bladder cancer (urothelial carcinoma) presenting with painless macroscopic haematuria and clot passage

pathophysiology_summary: Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder arises from carcinogen exposure — cigarette smoking is the dominant modifiable risk factor, delivering aromatic amines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons that are renally excreted and concentrated against the urothelium. Tumour neovascularisation is fragile, so the classic and often first presentation is PAINLESS macroscopic haematuria, which may be intermittent and can shed clots large enough to obstruct the bladder outlet (clot retention). Preceding irritative voiding symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency) can reflect carcinoma in situ and are frequently mislabelled as urinary tract infection, delaying diagnosis. Constitutional features (unintentional weight loss, fatigue) suggest higher-stage or more advanced disease. Any adult with painless visible haematuria requires urgent urological assessment with flexible cystoscopy, upper-tract imaging and urine cytology; the diagnosis cannot be made or excluded by telehealth history alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Painless visible haematuria with clots is urothelial malignancy until proven otherwise, especially in an older smoker.
Preceding irritative symptoms raise it, but painless bleeding and failed antibiotics argue against active infection.
Stones cause haematuria but almost always with colicky flank pain, which is absent here.
Must be actively excluded by medication history before attributing bleeding to a lesion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, painless gross haematuria with clots should already put urothelial malignancy at the top and trigger a fast-track workup mindset — not an infective anchor.

differential.evidence_basis: 40 pack-year smoking + painless bleeding + weeks of irritative symptoms + weight loss + fatigue is a textbook urothelial-cancer profile.

differential.diagnosis: upper-tract urothelial carcinoma / renal cell carcinoma
differential.evidence_basis: Same risk factors and haematuria can arise from the renal pelvis, ureter or kidney; imaging of the upper tracts is part of the same fast-track workup.
Antibiotic failure and painless bleeding make active infection unlikely as the sole cause — but it must not become the anchoring diagnosis.
No colicky flank pain and painless bleeding make a symptomatic stone very unlikely.

differential.diagnosis: bladder cancer (urothelial carcinoma) — presumptive, pending cystoscopy
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be confirmed remotely; flexible cystoscopy, upper-tract imaging and urine cytology are required. Telehealth-correct action is urgent fast-track referral, not a made diagnosis.

red_flags.clinical_significance: The single most important red flag for urothelial malignancy in an adult — mandates urgent urological referral and cystoscopy regardless of whether the bleeding stops.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling as another UTI delays cancer diagnosis, allowing progression to muscle-invasive or metastatic disease.

red_flags.description: Passage of large clots with risk of clot retention / bladder outlet obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Clots can occlude the bladder outlet causing acute painful retention needing emergency catheterisation and bladder washout, and heavy bleeding can cause haemodynamic compromise.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated clot retention leads to painful obstruction, acute kidney injury and possible bladder rupture.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss (~4 kg / 2 months) with profound fatigue
red_flags.clinical_significance: Constitutional symptoms raise concern for higher-stage or advanced malignancy and reinforce urgency of the workup.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to weight the constitutional features may lead to under-urgent referral.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Smoking is the dominant risk factor for urothelial carcinoma and sharply raises pre-test probability, mandating fast-track investigation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Underestimating pre-test probability risks a routine rather than urgent referral.

red_flags.description: Lightheadedness with fear of collapse in a patient who lives alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Possible early sign of significant blood loss / hypovolaemia; social isolation lowers the threshold for escalation because deterioration may go unwitnessed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A silent bleed or collapse at home could go unassisted.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Re-attributing the bleeding to a urinary infection because of the preceding irritative symptoms — despite painless bleeding and a failed antibiotic course. This is the classic delay-causing error in bladder cancer.
Being falsely reassured because the bleeding does not hurt. Painlessness is exactly what makes visible haematuria a malignancy red flag, not a benign feature.
Assuming that because the bleeding comes and goes it can wait. Intermittent painless visible haematuria still mandates urgent cystoscopy — it must be investigated even if it stops.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Telehealth over-reach — minting a diagnosis or procedure
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Declaring bladder cancer as certain, or instructing catheterisation/bladder washout/tranexamic acid, over telehealth. The AI can neither confirm the diagnosis nor perform the procedure; the correct output is urgent fast-track referral plus safety-netting.
Escalating a haemodynamically stable, still-voiding patient to an emergency 000 ambulance when an urgent same-day/next-day fast-track pathway with a hard safety-net is appropriate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and heart rate / true haemodynamic status (source: BP 115/75, HR 95 — clinician-measured)
Formal urinalysis / point-of-care dipstick (source showed florid macroscopic haematuria without nitrites or leukocytes)
Abdominal and suprapubic palpation for a mass or distended bladder (source: soft, non-tender, no palpable mass, mild suprapubic fullness)
Full blood count / haemoglobin, UEC and coagulation profile
Flexible cystoscopy (expected papillary mucosal mass) and upper-tract imaging (CT urogram / ultrasound)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise painless visible haematuria with clots as a fast-track urology red flag; arrange urgent (same-day/next-day) in-person GP review to generate an expedited urology/haematuria-clinic referral for flexible cystoscopy, plus urgent bloods (FBC/UEC/coags) and urine cytology. Give strong safety-netting: ED/000 immediately for inability to pass urine (clot retention), faintness/collapse, or heavy continuous bleeding.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate. The real risks are (a) falsely reassuring the patient as a UTI and under-triaging, and (b) the AI minting the bladder-cancer diagnosis as certain or ordering a procedure/drug it cannot deliver. The correct posture is high suspicion with an urgent in-person pathway, not a confirmed diagnosis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: General urological principle — painless visible haematuria pathway (candidate reasoning, pending clinician/guideline confirmation)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Painless visible (macroscopic) haematuria in an adult, particularly a current or ex-smoker, mandates urgent urological referral with flexible cystoscopy, upper-tract imaging and urine cytology to exclude urothelial malignancy, even if the bleeding is intermittent or has stopped.
